content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 15 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), LC PDF pages 212–213 | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus, pa_0153
**Verse count:** 23

---

**1** At the end of every seven years you shall make a release [shemittah].

**2** And this is the manner of the release: every creditor shall release what he has lent to his neighbor; he shall not press his neighbor and his brother, for the LORD's release has been proclaimed.

**3** Of a foreigner [nokri] you may press payment, but whatever of yours is with your brother your hand shall release.

**4** But there will be no poor among you—for the LORD will surely bless you in the land which the LORD your God is giving you as an inheritance to possess—

**5** if only you fully obey the voice of the LORD your God, being careful to do all this commandment that I am commanding you today.

**6** For the LORD your God will bless you, as he promised you, and you will lend to many nations but you will not borrow, and you will rule over many nations but they will not rule over you.

**7** If there is among you a poor man, one of your brothers, within any of your gates in your land which the LORD your God is giving you, you shall not harden your heart and you shall not shut your hand against your poor brother.

**8** Rather you shall surely open your hand to him and you shall surely lend him sufficient for his need in whatever he lacks.

**9** Take care lest there be a worthless thought in your heart, saying, "The seventh year, the year of release, draws near," so that your eye is hostile toward your poor brother and you give him nothing, and he cries to the LORD against you, and there is sin in you.

**10** You shall surely give to him, and your heart shall not be grieved when you give to him, for on account of this thing the LORD your God will bless you in all your work and in all that you put your hand to.

**11** For the poor will never cease from the land; therefore I am commanding you, saying, "You shall surely open your hand to your brother, to the needy and to the poor in your land."

**12** If your brother, a Hebrew man or a Hebrew woman, is sold to you and serves you six years, then in the seventh year you shall set him free from you.

**13** And when you set him free from you, you shall not send him out empty-handed.

**14** You shall furnish him liberally from your flock and from your threshing floor and from your winepress; as the LORD your God has blessed you, you shall give to him.

**15** And you shall remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt and the LORD your God redeemed you; therefore I am commanding you this thing today.

**16** And it shall be, if he says to you, "I will not go out from you," because he loves you and your household, since he is well-off with you—

**17** then you shall take the awl and put it through his ear into the door, and he shall be your servant forever. And also to your female servant you shall do likewise.

**18** Let it not seem hard to you when you set him free, for at double the wage of a hired worker he has served you six years; and the LORD your God will bless you in all that you do.

**19** Every firstborn male that is born in your herd and in your flock you shall consecrate to the LORD your God; you shall not work with the firstborn of your ox and you shall not shear the firstborn of your flock.

**20** Before the LORD your God you shall eat it year by year in the place which the LORD will choose, you and your household.

**21** But if there is in it a blemish—lameness or blindness, or any serious blemish—you shall not sacrifice it to the LORD your God.

**22** In your gates you shall eat it—the unclean and the clean alike—as you would eat the gazelle or the deer.

**23** Only its blood you shall not eat; on the ground you shall pour it out like water.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**v.1 — shemittah:** SOPHIA retains the Hebrew term *shemittah* with a bracketed gloss "release" on first occurrence, following project convention. This is the debt-release law; PNEUMA's "cancel debts" captures the meaning but is interpretively forward; EMET's and LOGOS's "release" preserves the broader concept.

**v.9 — "worthless thought" vs. "belial thing":** EMET preserves *belial* as a semi-technical term; LOGOS uses "worthless thought"; PNEUMA "wicked thought." SOPHIA adopts "worthless thought" (LOGOS) as clearest without technicality.

**v.11 — "afflicted and poor" vs. "needy and poor":** EMET distinguishes *anaw* ("afflicted") and *evyon* ("poor/needy"); LOGOS and PNEUMA use "needy and poor." SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "needy and poor" for natural English flow, noting the two terms are distinct in the Hebrew.

**v.14 — "surely adorn" vs. "furnish liberally":** EMET renders the cognate intensifier *ha'aneq ta'aniq* literally; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "furnish liberally" / "supply generously." SOPHIA adopts "furnish liberally" (LOGOS) as the clearest English for the emphatic construction.

**v.17 — "servant" vs. "slave":** LOGOS uses "slave" for *eved*; EMET and PNEUMA use "servant." SOPHIA uses "servant" in the translation body as the primary rendering, consistent with the earlier discussion of *eved ivri* in this passage as indentured service. See disagreements.

**v.18 — "double the wage" vs. "half the cost":** A significant textual disagreement between agents. EMET reads "at double the wage of a hired worker," LOGOS "at half the cost of a hired worker." The Hebrew *mishneh* can mean "double" (twice the value given) or be read differently. SOPHIA follows EMET's "double the wage" as the more natural reading of *mishneh sekir sakir* — the servant has been worth twice a hired hand's cost over six years. See disagreements.

**LXX note:** The Vaticanus (p. 153) contains the whole of ch. 15. Closely aligned with MT throughout; LXX renders *ha'aneq ta'aniq* with a cognate Greek construction.

*— SOPHIA*
